segments.s293-g121: یکی بندگی کردم is an older poetic clause meaning 'I did one act of servitude'. Here یکی (a single / one) modifies بندگی (servitude/devotion), and کردَم is the simple past first‑person verb 'I did/performed'. ای شهریار is a vocative 'O king' addressing the addressee after the statement.
segments.s293-g122: که introduces a relative clause ('which/that') modifying the preceding deed. ماند is the verb 'remains/lasts' (here 'remains'), followed by ز من 'from me' (ز is the poetic form of از). در جهان یادگار places that remainder 'in the world as a memorial' (در جهان = 'in the world'; یادگار = 'memorial/memento').
segments.s293-g123: بناهای آباد is a noun phrase 'prosperous/inhabited buildings' (بناهای = 'buildings', آباد = 'prosperous/inhabited'). گردد خراب uses گردَد (classical form of 'become') plus the predicate خراب 'ruined', so 'become ruined' (an intransitive stative/resultative construction common in literary Persian).
segments.s293-g124: ز باران وز تابش آفتاب gives the causes: 'from rain and from the shining of the sun'. Here the first ز = 'from'. The written وز is actually the contraction of و + ز (= 'and from') often used in poetry; تابش آفتاب = 'the sun's radiance/sunlight'.
segments.s293-g125: پی here means 'after/following' (linking this action to what came before). افگندم is the archaic past first person of افکندن used poetically to mean 'I erected/established' (literally 'I cast/laid down' in older diction). از نظم ('according to/from the verse/poem') qualifies the manner or inspiration; کاخی بلند is the object 'a lofty palace' (کاخ + ـی indefinite suffix, بلند = 'lofty').
segments.s293-g126: که introduces a relative clause modifying کاخی بلند. از باد و بارانش = 'from wind and its rain' (the suffix -ش on باران refers back to the palace: 'its rain' ≈ 'rain that affects it'), and نآید گزند is a negative potential/optative sense 'may no harm come' or 'let no harm come' (ن‑ + آید = 'does/should not come'); گزند = 'harm'.
segments.s293-g127: بر این نامه‌بر سالها بگذرد is a poetic inversion: بر ('upon') + این نامه‌بر ('this letter‑carrier' or 'this epistle/messenger') with سالها ('years') as the logical subject and بگذرد ('will pass') as the impersonal verb — 'years will pass over/upon this letter‑bearer'. The verb بگذرد is a present/future‑oriented classical form meaning 'pass (by)'.
segments.s293-g128: همی خواند is a habitual/continuous construction ('will keep reading' or 'continually reads'); همی marks ongoingness. آن کس که دارد خرد is a relative clause 'that person who has wisdom' = 'whoever has sense/wisdom' (دارد is present 'has', خرد = 'wisdom'), so the whole line says the wise will keep reading it.
segments.s293-g49: که is a relative/explanatory particle here ('for' / 'that'); آرایش is a noun 'adornment' serving as the predicate; چرخ گردنده is a noun phrase where چرخ = 'sky/heaven' is modified by گردنده ('rotating', present participle). The copula اوست ('he is') comes at the end, so the whole clause means 'that he is the adornment of the revolving sky.' Note the classical/poetic omission of an explicit ezāfe between آرایش and چرخ گردنده.
segments.s293-g50: به here marks location/setting ('at' or 'in'); بزم اندرون = 'the inner feast/assembly' (بزم 'banquet' + اندرون 'inner, within'); ابر بخشنده is a noun phrase where بخشنده ('generous', adjectival/agent sense) qualifies ابر ('cloud'); the clause ends with the copula اوست ('he is'), yielding 'in the inner banquet he is the generous cloud.' Word order places the locative phrase and descriptive noun phrase before the copula, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s293-g51: خرد هستش = 'there is wisdom' where هستش is the colloquial/poetic copula ('is'); the following nouns نیکنامی and داد are linked by و and share the same implied copula, so the line lists virtues ('wisdom, good name, and justice exist'). The short copula after the first noun governs the whole series.
segments.s293-g52: جهان is the subject 'the world'; بی‌سر 'without a head / leaderless' and افسر او ('his afsar' — here 'afsar' functions as a crown/diadem/helmet with the possessive pronoun او) form the predicate complement; مباد is an archaic optative/negative jussive meaning 'may it not be' (wishing that the world not be deprived). The clause therefore means 'may the world never be headless and without his crown.'
segments.s293-g53: A simple nominal list (سپاه و دل و گنج و دستور) followed by هست ('there is/are'). In Persian poetry the copula can follow a long list; دل is 'heart' (emotion/inner life), دستور here means 'order/administration' or 'command', and the whole phrase asserts the presence/existence of these things.
segments.s293-g54: همان (= 'the same' / 'likewise') links the following series as parallel to the previous list. رزم = 'battle', بزم = 'banquet/feast'. می‌و is a classical form related to pleasure/fruit/wine (here figurative, meaning indulgence/pleasure), and سور = 'feast/revelry'. The clause ends with هست, repeating the existential assertion. The structure is a parallel list introduced by همان.
segments.s293-g55: یکی here means 'a certain' / 'one' and modifies فرش ('carpet'); گسترده شد is the past passive/causative construction 'was spread' (گسترده 'spread-out' + شد 'became/was'), and در جهان gives the location 'in the world'. Word order places the indefinite determiner before the noun, then the verb and the locative phrase.
segments.s293-g56: که introduces a relative clause modifying فرش; هرگز 'never' negates the following verb; نشانش is 'its mark/trace' (نشان + possessive suffix -ش); نگردد is the negative subjunctive/jussive of گشتن 'to become' (here 'may not become'), and نهان is the adjective 'hidden'. The sequence yields 'whose mark may never become hidden.'
segments.s293-g41: که is the subordinating/complementizer 'that' (linking this clause to the preceding context). در بزم گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'in the world's assembly/banquet' (بزم + گیتی, noun-noun). بدو is an indirect object pronoun 'to him' (not the imperative), and روشن است is the predicate 'is bright/clear' with the copula است; word order places the indirect object before the predicate for poetic effect.
segments.s293-g42: به رزم is a temporal/locative phrase 'in battle' (به here introducing the circumstance). اندرون کوه 'the inner part of the mountain' uses اندرون = 'inside/within' followed by the noun کوه. در جوشن است literally 'is in/covered by armor' (در functioning as 'in' + جوشن 'armor'), so the verb است predicates that the mountain's interior is clad in mail; the line shows poetic inversion of normal prose order.
segments.s293-g43: A noun phrase naming the person: ابوالقاسم (a kunya/personal name) followed by آن شهریار دلیر 'that brave shah/ruler'. آن here is the demonstrative/definite marker 'that/the', and دلیر is a postpositive adjective modifying شهریار.
segments.s293-g44: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where'. گور is the object 'grave'. بستاند here is the finite verb meaning 'will lay/establish/dig' (classical-poetic present/future/subjunctive sense); the provided senses do not match this verbal sense, so index -1 is used. از چنگ شیر is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the lion's claw' with از = 'from', چنگ = 'claw', شیر = 'lion'.
segments.s293-g45: جهاندار محمود is the nominal subject 'sovereign Mahmud' (جهاندار = 'sovereign/conqueror', محمود = a proper name). کاندر نبرد is a fused archaic expression meaning 'in which battle' / 'in that battle' (کاندر = 'in which/therein' modifying نبرد 'battle').
segments.s293-g46: سر سرکشان is the object phrase 'the heads of the proud/arrogant ones' (سر = 'head', سرکشان = plural/agent noun 'the proud/rebellious' — the listed lemma/senses do not match this poetic adjective, so -1 is given for that item). اندر آرد is the verbal phrase 'brings into' (اندر = 'into/among', آرد = 'he brings'). به گرد is a locative/phrase 'to/into the circle/around' (به + گرد 'around'). Word order is poetic and compact.
segments.s293-g47: جهان تا جهان is a customary hyperbolic formula 'so long as the world (exists)' with تا = 'until/as long as'. باشد is the subjunctive/optative form 'may he be' (classical usage), او شاه is 'he (be) king', and باد is the optative particle 'may' reinforcing the wish; together they form the blessing 'May he be king as long as the world lasts.'
segments.s293-g48: بلند is an epithet 'lofty/high'. اخترش is اختر 'star' with the enclitic possessive -ش 'his' (i.e. 'his star'). افسر ماه is a noun phrase 'the moon's diadem/helmet' (افسر = 'helmet/diadem', ماه = 'moon'), and باد again is the optative particle 'may it be'. The clause wishes that his high star be the moon's crown.
segments.s293-g105: This is a string of epithets modifying the ruler: noun first ('خداوند' = lord/sovereign) followed by adjectival descriptors. 'زیبای' is an adjectival/poetic form meaning 'beautiful' qualifying the noun; 'برترمنش' is an epithet meaning someone of a superior or proud disposition. Persian permits several adjectives in sequence after the noun in poetry.
segments.s293-g106: 'از او دور' is a prepositional phrase 'far from him'; what follows ('پیغاره و سرزنش') are two nouns listed as being remote from him. The verb/copula is omitted (a common poetic device) so the idea is 'messages and reproaches are far from him' or 'he is beyond messages and blame.'
segments.s293-g107: Poetic inversion: the verb 'بدرد' (here an archaic/poetic verbal form) comes before the phrase 'ز آواز او' and the subject 'کوه سنگ' follows; the line means that the mountain of stone is affected (rends/shakes/splits) by his voice. This is an old poetic usage of word order and a verb sense not fully covered by the supplied glosses.
segments.s293-g108: Parallel locative phrases with omitted copula: 'به دریا نهنگ' and 'به خشکی پلنگ' mean 'in/at the sea (there is) a whale' and 'on the land (there is) a leopard.' 'به' here functions idiomatically to mark the location/context (in/at).
segments.s293-g109: The correlative 'چه ... چه ...' functions as 'whether ... or ...' (setting up a contrast). 'در پیش بزمش' means 'in front of his feast' or 'before his banquet' (i.e., in his presence). The two nouns ('دینار' and 'خاک') are the contrasted items.
segments.s293-g110: 'ز بخشش' marks the relevant respect/cause ('with regard to giving' or 'because of generosity'). The verb 'ندارد' (does not have) follows, with the possessed noun 'دلش' (his heart) before it — a common inverted order in Persian poetry — giving 'his heart has no fear.' The enclitic 'ش' is the possessive 'his.'
segments.s293-g111: A sequence of titles and proper name: 'جهاندار' (sovereign) + personal name 'محمود' + epithet 'خورشیدفش'. Such title strings are nominative compounds; 'خورشیدفش' here behaves as an epithet/epithetic name.
segments.s293-g112: 'به رزم' = 'in battle' (prepositional phrase). What follows are two coordinated predicates/epithets describing him in that context: 'اندرون شیر' = 'a lion within (i.e., in battle)' and 'شمشیرکش' = 'a sword‑drawer/swordsman.' The copula is omitted, so the line reads as an assertion of his martial qualities.
segments.s293-g17: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he'. مرز is the direct object meaning 'frontier/edge'. گیرد (literary present) is 3sg of گرفتن 'take/hold' and here describes the action of assuming the front; به + instrument (به شمشیر تیز) marks the means 'with a sharp sword'. Word order is subordinating clause: [چو + subject + object + verb + instrument].
segments.s293-g18: بر انگیزد is a verbal phrase: بر is a prefix/particle that combines with انگیزد (3sg of انگیختن 'to arouse/raise') to mean 'raises/arouses'. اندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/throughout the world'. رستخیز is the noun object/complement meaning a great 'resurrection' or 'uprising'. The verb is transitive and the clause states the resulting effect in the larger world.
segments.s293-g19: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. دشمن is the source 'the enemy'. ستاند here is a verbal form meaning 'takes/snatches' (classical/epic usage); none of the supplied senses matched exactly so a specific lexical sense is unavailable in the provided list. ببخشد is 3sg of بخشیدن with the prefix بـ marking the imperfective/presentive 'gives/bestows' and is directed 'به دوست' ('to the friend'), so the clause has two coordinated verbs: takes (from enemy) and gives (to friend).
segments.s293-g20: This is a nominal sentence with copula at the end. خداوند is 'God' (or 'the Lord') and پیروزگر is an epithet 'victorious' functioning as an adjective/appositive. یار is 'ally/companion'. اوست (او + ست) is the literary copula 'he/it is', so the whole clause states that the victorious Lord is his ally.
segments.s293-g21: بدان is formed from the prefix بـ plus دان (دان here = 'know'); together functioning as an imperative/introductory 'know that' or 'be aware that'. تیغزن is a compound adjective 'sword-striking' (one who strikes with a sword). دست is 'hand' (literal or power/arm). گوهرفشان is a descriptive compound 'gem-scattering' or 'gem-glittering', modifying the hand. The phrase is a topical noun phrase introduced by 'بدان'.
segments.s293-g22: ز is again the poetic preposition 'from' / 'in respect of' and گیتی is 'the world'. نجوید is the negative present of جستن 'to seek' (here 'seeks not' / 'does not ask for'), with the archaic adverbial همی adding continuity/emphasis ('ever/always'). جز is the preposition 'except' and نشان is the thing sought/desired 'a sign/token' (i.e. a trophy or mark). The clause states that the hand desires nothing from the world except a token.
segments.s293-g23: که begins a relative clause 'who/that'. در is the locative preposition 'in'. بزم is 'banquet/assembly' (poetic), and دریایش literally 'his sea' (دریا + possessive -ش), here a grand image 'his oceanic banquet/assembly'. خواند is 3sg of خواندن used in the sense 'to call/summon' (literary); سپهر 'the sky/heavens' is the object. Word order is literary: relative + locative phrase + verb + object.
segments.s293-g24: به رزم is a directional/goal phrase 'to/for battle'. اندرون is 'within/inner' and here links to شیر 'lion' (an epithet or image brought into combat). خورشید چهر is a compound adjective 'sun-faced' (خورشید 'sun' + چهر 'face/appearance') describing the lion. The clause is a compact poetic image: in war he brings forward an inner lion with a face like the sun.
segments.s293-g25: گواهی دهد is a verbal phrase meaning 'gives testimony' (دهد is the third-person singular literary/present form). بر governs the locative phrase that follows ('on/upon'), so بر زمین means 'on the earth'; خاک و آب are coordinated nouns specifying what on the earth is meant ('soil and water'). Word order is typical literary Persian: verb follows its nominal phrase and the preposition introduces the location.
segments.s293-g26: همان here means 'the same' or 'likewise' and links this prepositional phrase to the previous one; بر فلک means 'upon the sky/heavens'. چشمه آفتاب is a genitive/construct: 'the spring (source) of the sun' (classical Persian often juxtaposes two nouns to show possession), so the clause parallels the earlier 'on earth ...' construction.
segments.s293-g27: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('that' / 'for'); چون او = 'like him'. ندیدست is an archaic/literary perfect/passive construction meaning 'has not been seen' (the negative sense is expressed by the form); شاهی here means 'a king'/'kingship' in the sense 'a ruler of that kind'. به جنگ means 'in battle' and completes the clause: 'a king like him has not been seen in war'.
segments.s293-g28: The segment begins (implicitly) with نه, a negation that scopes over the list: 'not in ...'. در governs the following nouns: بخشش و کوشش و نام و ننگ — four coordinated nouns giving the spheres in which no one equals him. Note the repeated و simply links items in the list; the negation applies to the whole series ('not in generosity and effort and in name and shame').
segments.s293-g29: اگر introduces a conditional clause. مهر با کین برآمیزدی literally 'if love mingled with hatred': با = 'with', and برآمیزدی is an archaic optative/irrealis form (the -دی ending marks a counterfactual / hypothetical), here 'were to mingle' or 'should mingle'. The clause expresses a hypothetical contrary-to-fact condition.
segments.s293-g30: This is the result clause of the conditional: ستاره (the stars/as a subject) + ز (poetic form of از = 'from/because of') خشمش ('his anger' — خشم + enclitic ش). بپرهیزدی uses the same optative/irrealis ending -دی and means 'would avoid' or 'would shy away'. Word order places the verb at the end of the clause, as normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s293-g31: تنش is تن + the possessive suffix ش 'his' = 'his body'. زورمندست is an adjectival predicate with the copula -ست ('is powerful'). و introduces the next coordinated phrase: چندان سپاه = 'so many/such an army' (چندان 'to that degree' modifies سپاه), i.e. 'and an army so great'. The sequence contrasts his own strength with the size of his forces.
segments.s293-g32: که begins a result clause 'so that/that'. اندر میان = 'in the midst of' (اندر = in/within, میان = middle/midst). باد را (باد with the object marker را) indicates 'for the wind' or 'as for the wind' — topicalized/definite — and نیست راه = 'there is no path/way' (literary word order places نیست before راه). The whole clause says the army is so dense that there is no way through even for the wind.
segments.s293-g137: دلش is دل + the enclitic possessive ش meaning “his” (noun + suffix). شادمانه here functions adjectivally/adverbially (‘joyful’/‘joyfully’) describing the state of the heart; the phrase چو خرم بهار is a simile introduced by چو (‘like/as’). خرم qualifies بهار as ‘lush/cheerful’ (a poetic epithet for spring). Word order is poetic (possessed noun + predicate + simile).
segments.s293-g138: همیشه is an adverb ‘always/ever’ that scopes over the following prepositional phrase. بر این گردش روزگار is a prepositional phrase where بر = ‘upon/throughout’, این = ‘this’, گردش روزگار is a genitive-like collocation meaning ‘the turning/cycle of time’ (poetic omission of ezāfe). The whole phrase means ‘ever during this turning of time.’
segments.s293-g139: از او introduces a causal/source phrase here (‘because of him / from him’). شادمانه is adverbial (‘joyfully’ or ‘rejoiced’) and modifies the omitted/copulative predicate (classically the verb بود is often omitted), producing ‘the assembly’s heart was joyful because of him.’ دل انجمن is a reversed order for ‘the heart of the assembly’ (poetic ellipsis of an ezāfe).
segments.s293-g140: به هر کار is a locative/limiting phrase ‘in/for every deed/task’ (به functioning as ‘in/with regard to’ + هر ‘every’ + کار ‘deed’). پیروز و چیره سخن are predicative adjectives/epithets: پیروز ‘victorious’ and چیره (here ‘dominant/skilled’) qualifying (the implied subject) in regard to سخن ‘speech/wit’ — again the copula is omitted by poetic style, so ‘(he was) victorious in every task and masterful in speech.’
segments.s293-g141: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning ‘still/continually’ and here helps set a temporal clause. تا is the conjunction ‘as long as / until’, introducing the subordinate temporal clause. بگردد is the classical present/subjunctive form of گشت/گردیدن ‘to turn’ (here ‘revolves/shall turn’), so the clause reads ‘as long as the heavens turn.’ فلک is the subject ‘the sky/heaven’ and چرخ‌وار is an adverb/adjectival compound ‘wheel-like / rotating’ modifying how the heavens turn.
segments.s293-g142: بود is the copula/existential verb ‘was/there was’. اندر او = ‘within him / in him’ (اندر = in/within + او = him). مشتری را places مشتری (Jupiter, the planet) with the Persian definite/direct-object marker را; گذار here is a poetic noun meaning ‘placement/station/influence’ (word order is archaic: copula + location + object with را). The overall sense is ‘Jupiter’s station/influence was in him.’
segments.s293-g143: شهنشاه ما is a possessive phrase ‘our king’ (ما here functions as the possessive ‘our’ in poetic usage). باد is the optative particle meaning ‘may (he be)’ — it expresses a wish rather than the noun ‘wind’. با جاه و ناز is an instrumental/accompaniment phrase ‘with rank and nâz’ (ناز here is a poetic notion of grace/pride/dignity). The whole segment is a benediction: ‘May our king be with rank and dignity.’
segments.s293-g144: از او دور introduces ‘from him / away from him’ where از + او marks separation/source and دور = ‘far’ modifies what follows. چشم بد is a compound meaning ‘evil eye’ (چشم + بد). The trailing و بی‌نیاز is an added wish/adjective phrase ‘and (may he be) without need’ (بی‌نیاز = self-sufficient); the copula (‘may it be’/‘may he be’) is again omitted and supplied by the optative sense of the previous باد.
segments.s293-g153: کنون is an adverb placed up front meaning 'now.' خامه‌ای is an indefinite noun (خامه + ی) 'a pen' or 'a writing-style' and is the object of یافتم. یافتم is past first-person 'I found/obtained' (verb at or near the end is normal in classical Persian). بیش از آن is a comparative phrase 'more than that' modifying what was found; grammatically it attaches to خامه‌ای and links into the following relative clause.
segments.s293-g154: که introduces a relative/clausal phrase 'than that which...' (here 'that which I had done earlier'). مغز سخن is a genitive construction 'the core/essence of speech' (مغز = core). بافتم here is the past first-person of بافتن 'I wove/constructed/composed' and closes the clause (verb-final). پیش از آن = 'before that'; پیش governs the temporal relation and از آن repeats the demonstrative 'that.' Note: the lemma/senses given for بافتم in the dataset do not match the actual verbal root بافتن, so its sense is irregular here.

segments.s293-g156: گهی ... گهی is a pair of adverbs meaning 'sometimes ... sometimes ...' that set up an alternation. شادمانی is 'joy' and stands as one pole; درد و خشم are the other pole 'pain and anger.' The clause lacks an explicit verb here (elliptical): these noun phrases specify what the 'two eyes' in the previous segment alternately behold.
segments.s293-g157: شگفت functions as an exclamatory/predicative 'it is wondrous/strange' and introduces the following clause. اندر این گنبد لاژورد is a locative prepositional phrase 'within this lapis-lazuli dome' (اندر = in; the adjective لاژورد follows the noun as a color/material modifier).
segments.s293-g158: بماند is the third-person form of ماندن used here to mean 'remains/should remain' (classical narrative/optative shading is common). چنین 'such' modifies the following noun phrase. دل پر از داغ و درد = 'a heart full of burning grief and sorrow' where پر از = 'full of' follows the noun and links to the nouns داغ (burning grief) and درد (sorrow).
segments.s293-g159: چنین بود 'it was thus' is a past narrative statement. تا introduces a temporal limit 'until/while'; the repeated بود emphasizes duration. دور زمان = 'a distant (long) time' i.e. for a long period — the clause says this state persisted for a long span.
segments.s293-g160: به نوی تو 'with regard to your newness/novelty' (به = toward/with respect to; نوی = new/novel; تو = you). اندر شگفتی ممان is an admonition 'do not remain in astonishment' — ممان is a classical/poetic negated or prohibitive form related to ماندن 'to remain' (the dataset's glosses for ممان do not reflect this imperative/negation sense, so its exact sense index is not available). The whole segment is a warning: do not be kept in wonder by your newness.
segments.s293-g57: کجا (interrogative/adverb) introduces a locative clause: “where.” فرش را uses را as the topical/definite marker on “the carpet.” مسند و مرقد است is a nominal predicate: two nouns joined by و (and) followed by the copula است (“is”), so literally “the carpet is a throne and a tomb/resting‑place.”
segments.s293-g58: نشستنگه is a compound noun meaning “seat / place of sitting.” It forms a predicative/genitival phrase with the following name: نصر بن احمد است = “is (i.e. belongs to) Nasr ibn Ahmad.” The copula است closes the clause. (بن here functions as the patronymic link “son of,” not covered by the provided sense list.)
segments.s293-g59: که is a subordinating/linking particle (“that / for”), introducing a clause giving a reason or description. این‌گونه آرام شاهی is a noun phrase “such calm/peaceful kingship.” بدوست here is a contracted or idiomatic form meaning “is liked/pleasing” (compound of به + دوست in older usage) and serves as the predicate expressing that the kingship is pleasing/esteemed.
segments.s293-g60: خرد در سر نامداران is a prepositional phrase meaning “wisdom in the head/mind of the renowned.” در is the preposition “in,” and سر here carries the idiomatic sense “inner mind / thought.” نکوست is the copular predicate “is good/fitting,” completing the clause.
segments.s293-g61: نبد is an archaic negative-existential (literary) form meaning “there was not / there did not exist.” خسروان را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker on “kings.” چنو means “such as / like,” so the phrase states that kings like a کدخدای (village headman) did not exist — i.e. none equalled a village‑headman in these qualities.
segments.s293-g62: بپرهیز is the present/habitual verbal form of پرهیزیدن (“to refrain / to guard”), here meaning “he kept/observed (religion).” دین is the object “religion.” به + رادی و رای expresses manner/qualities “with generosity (رادی) and with good counsel/judgement (رای)” — به is the prepositional marker “by/with/through,” رادی is “generous,” and رای here means “counsel/decision.”
segments.s293-g63: A string of coordinated adjectival/nominal descriptions: گشاده‌زبان (“open‑tongued, frank”), و linking, دل (“heart,” used here as ‘open‑hearted’), و linking, پاک دست (“clean‑handed,” i.e. honest). The nouns/compounds are conjoined by و and function as a single descriptive phrase for the subject described in the next segment.
segments.s293-g64: پرستنده is a present‑participle/noun “worshipper / devotee” used as a predicate/noun of description. شاه here is the noun “king” or “ruler,” and یزدان‌پرست is a compound adjective/noun meaning “God‑worshipping / devout.” Together this segment gives a characterization: “(a) worshipper, a God‑worshipping king/man,” with the participle and the compound forming appositional qualities.
segments.s293-g1: ز + یزدان marks source ('from God'); بر آن شاه places the beneficiary/target ('upon that king') with the preposition بر. باد is the classical optative particle 'may there be' and governs the following noun آفرین, which here is a noun 'praise/acclamation' (not the verb 'to create'). Word order is source — recipient — optative — noun: 'From God upon that king may there be praise.'
segments.s293-g2: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who/for'), linking to the preceding prayer. نازد is the present/poetic form of نازیدن 'to be proud/boast' (here used as a predicate); بدو is the dative 'to him' (about him). The items تاج و تخت و نگین are the logical subjects/agents that 'take pride' in him; classical poetry often places the verb before its subjects, so the clause reads 'the crown, the throne, and the jewel take pride in him.'
segments.s293-g3: که gives a causal/explanatory sense ('for/because'). گنجش (poetic form of 'گنج' with possessive sense) is the subject 'his treasure/treasury'; ز بخشش 'from/because of (his) giving' expresses cause. بنالد is present/poetic 'moans/laments' and همی is the habitual/continuous particle meaning 'continually/keeps'. The clause reads 'for his treasure moans because of his generosity.'
segments.s293-g4: بزرگی ('greatness') is the subject; ز نامش 'from his name' = 'because of his reputation' (note ش is the possessive 'his'). ببالد is the present form meaning 'takes pride / is proud' and همی again gives a continuous sense. Word order is subject — adjunct — verb — particle: 'his greatness, because of his name, is proud.'
segments.s293-g5: ز دریا به دریا is an idiomatic 'from X to Y' construction meaning 'from sea to sea' (span of extent). سپاه وی است is a nominal clause with the possessor/agent reported by وی ('his' / 'he') and the copula است at the end: 'his army is (from sea to sea).' The copula final position and the prepositional extent at the front are typical of classical Persian poetic word order.
segments.s293-g6: This is a vivid metaphor: جهان زیر فر 'the world under the far' (فر = royal/divine glory) states the relation 'the world is beneath his glory.' کلاه وی است literally 'is his cap/hat' — i.e. the world lies under his glory as if it were his cap. Word order places the locative/relational phrase before the copula, and وی marks possession/emphasis.
segments.s293-g7: خداوند is used as a title 'lord/sovereign' (repeated for emphasis) and is linked in a paratactic list to two domains: نام 'name/reputation' and گنج 'treasure.' The repetition of خداوند before each noun is a poetic device enumerating the things over which he is lord.
segments.s293-g8: The pattern continues: خداوند introduces another group of domains. شمشیر 'sword' names martial power; خفتان (here a nominal sense 'sleep/rest') and رنج 'suffering/hardship' round out the list. The ands (و) simply join the items in the enumeration.
segments.s293-g33: پس is a sentence-initial particle meaning 'then/therefore'. لشکرش is a noun + possessive enclitic (-ش) meaning 'his army/host'. The numeral phrase هفتصد ژنده پیل follows the possessor and describes that army: هفتصد (seven hundred) modifies ژنده پیل, an archaic/poetic compound 'ژنده پیل' literally 'ragged/shaggy elephants' (an epithet for elephants). Word order is particle → possessor → numeral + noun phrase.
segments.s293-g34: خدای جهان is a genitive phrase 'God of the world' (خدای + جهان). یارش is یار (ally/helper) with the enclitic -ش 'his', so 'his ally'; the copula is omitted (typical in classical Persian) so this phrase functions as a nominative apposition or predicate to the previous clause. و جبرییل simply adds 'and Gabriel' as a second supporter, coordinated by و.
segments.s293-g35: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/always' placed before the verb phrase for emphasis. باژ here denotes the tribute/levy (an older poetic word); خواهد is the present/future-form of خواستن used as 'wants/will demand' — the light verb follows the noun (typical noun + خواهد construction). ز هر مهتری is a prepositional phrase 'from every chief' (ز = 'from', هر = 'every', مهتری = 'chief/elder').
segments.s293-g36: This is a continuation in parallel: ز هر ... و هر ... repeats the preposition and particle هر for emphasis. نامداری and کشوری are nouns rendered here as 'every notable/famed one' and 'every country/realm' respectively; the coordinate و links the two objects of 'demand'.
segments.s293-g37: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). باژ ندهند is the protasis: باژ (tribute) + ندهند (negative present/imperative-like form of دادن) meaning 'they do not/they will not give (tribute)'. کشور دهند is the apodosis: کشور (the country/realm) + دهند ('they give' / 'they surrender') — verb at the end is normal for classical Persian.
segments.s293-g38: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the very', marking what follows as the thing given. گنج is 'treasure' (direct object). هم is an adverb 'also/too' attached before تخت and افسر (throne and helmet), which are coordinated by و, and the concluding دهد/دهند (here دهند) 'they give' applies to the whole list.
segments.s293-g39: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'. یارد here means 'ally/helper' and is the person affected by the action. گذشتن ز پیمان literally 'to pass beyond the covenant' is an idiomatic classical expression meaning 'to break or transgress the pact' (گذشتن 'to pass' used figuratively). اوی is the archaic enclitic 'his' referring back to the ruler; the whole phrase describes one who violates the oath with his ally.
segments.s293-g40: و‌گر is a conditional 'and if / but if'. سر کشیدن is an idiom ('to raise the head') meaning to rebel or resist; here it takes ز + فرمان 'against the command' (ز = 'from/against', فرمان = 'command'). اوی again is the possessive 'his'. The clause therefore means 'or if he raises his head in defiance of his command.'
segments.s293-g65: ز is a preposition meaning 'from' or 'by' (poetic). دستور is the noun 'decree/command' and is modified by the two adjectives فرزانه and دادگر that follow it; و simply joins the two adjectives ('wise and just'). The whole phrase means 'from the wise and just decree'.
segments.s293-g66: پراگنده (scattered/widespread) precedes the noun رنج (suffering), forming 'widespread suffering'; من is the possessor ('my') attached after the noun; آمد is past 3rd‑person singular 'came' and the phrase آمد به بر (came to/upon) is an idiom meaning 'befell/came upon (me)'.
segments.s293-g67: بپیوستم is 1st‑person singular past 'I joined/attached'. این نامه باستان = 'this ancient letter' where باستان is the adjective 'ancient' placed after the noun (typical Persian order).
segments.s293-g68: پسندیده is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'approved / chosen' modifying the preceding 'نامه'; از introduces the source 'from the دفتر (book)'; راستان is the genitive 'of the righteous' (i.e. 'the book of the righteous').
segments.s293-g69: که introduces a subordinate/result clause ('that'); تا روز پیری is a temporal phrase 'until the day of (my) old age'; مرا is the object 'me' (here split in the tokenization as م + را), and بر دهد is the verb complex where دهد (3rd‑singular 'gives/bestows') takes مرا as its object — overall 'that... he would give me (until my old age)'.
segments.s293-g70: A simple coordinated list of things given: بزرگی 'greatness', دینار 'dinar(s)/money', و افسر 'and a helmet' followed by the verb دهد repeated (classical/narrative use of حاضر 3rd‑singular to express giving/wishing). The verb here conveys bestowal/granting.
segments.s293-g71: ندیدم is negative past 1st‑person 'I did not see'; جهاندار is the noun 'sovereign / world‑ruler' and بخشنده‌ای is an indefinite adjectival form 'a generous/munificent one' following the noun (the -ای ending marks indefiniteness). Together: 'I have not seen a generous sovereign.'
segments.s293-g72: به تخت کیان is a locative phrase 'on the throne of the Kians' (به + تخت + genitive کیان). بر here functions as an intensifying/relational particle with درخشنده‌ای ('resplendent one'), yielding 'so/more resplendent on the Kian throne' — i.e. 'so resplendent upon the Kian throne.'
segments.s293-g177: نیابیم is the negative verb form (ن + یابیم) meaning 'we do not/find' (here functioning as 'we cannot find'); the verb appears before its complements, a common poetic order. بر چرخ گردنده is a prepositional phrase ('on the revolving wheel/heaven') modifying راه, which comes at the end: literally 'we do-not-find on (the) revolving heaven a way.'
segments.s293-g178: نه is the coordinating negation 'nor' linking this phrase to the preceding negative; بر governs کار ('on/depending on the work'). دادار is an epithet for the Creator/God and خورشید و ماه are in apposition as examples of that 'work' — 'not on the Creator's work, the sun and moon.'
segments.s293-g179: جهاندار is the subject 'the world-ruler/sovereign.' اگر چند here introduces a concessive sense ('though/even if'), with چند softening the concession. کوشد is third-person singular present 'strives/struggles,' and به رنج is an adjunct of manner/condition 'with suffering / in toil.'
segments.s293-g180: بتازد is a present verb 'he rushes/attacks' (verb-first order). به کین indicates the motive/attitude 'with hatred / in vengeance.' و connects the next clause; بنازد is a poetic present of نازیدن meaning 'he takes pride / boasts' and is governed by به گنج 'in regard to treasure.'
segments.s293-g181: The phrase uses an inverted word order: همش (all of it) + رفت (go) + باید (must) + به دیگر سرای (to another house/abode). Read idiomatically as 'all of it must go to another realm.' باید is the modal 'must/ought to.'
segments.s293-g182: بماند is the present subjunctive/optative 'remains / is left' (verb-first, poetic ordering). همه کوشش is the theme 'all effort,' ایدر means 'here,' and به جای 'in place' — together: 'all effort remains here in place.'
segments.s293-g183: تو is the vocative 'you.' از کار کیخسرو is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of the deeds/work of Kai Khosrow.' اندازه گیر is an imperative/instructional collocation 'take measure / consider / judge' (اندازه + the verb گیر 'take'), addressing the addressee to weigh those deeds.
segments.s293-g184: کهن گشته is a participial phrase 'has grown old / become ancient.' کار جهان is 'the world's work/affair.' تازه گیر repeats the verb گیر in an imperative sense with تازه 'new' — 'seize/take the new' (i.e., take up the new task). The clause contrasts the finished/old business with the call to assume the new work.
segments.s293-g145: کنون ز این سپس is an archaic/poetic fronting meaning “now, from this time onward.” ز (preposition) + این سپس (= “this then / henceforth”) indicates start point. نامه باستان is a noun phrase “the ancient book/record,” with نامه (book/record) modified by باستان (ancient). Word order is typical poetic Persian: temporal phrase first, then the object (نامه باستان).
segments.s293-g146: بپیوندم is a first‑person verb form with the prefixed b- (present/iterative aspect in classical/poetic usage) and the -م ending: “I join / I attach / I turn to.” از marks the source (“from”), گفته is ‘what is said’ or ‘accounts/statements,’ and راستان (“the truthful/upright”) qualifies گفته. The sequence means “I join/attach (my account) from the truthful statements,” i.e. “I base it on true reports.”
segments.s293-g147: چو here = “when.” پیش آورم is a light‑verb phrase: پیش (forward/before) + آورم (I bring/relate), so چو پیش آورم گردش روزگار = “when I bring forward/relate the turning (گردش) of روزگار (fate/time).” The verb آورم is first‑person singular, placed after its object phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s293-g148: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning roughly “it should not be / it is not necessary” (here best rendered “I need not / I do not require”). مرا (me) is split into م + را in the segmentation (م representing the object “me”); را is the definite/object marker attached to that object. پند آموزگار = “the teacher’s advice / admonition.” Together the clause means “I do not need a teacher’s admonition” (literally, “it should not be for me, the teacher’s admonition”).
segments.s293-g149: چو = “when.” This clause uses a simple past narrative: پیکار کیخسرو آمد پدید = “the battle of Kay Khosrow came and appeared” (آمد + پدید together mean ‘came into view / arose’). Word order places the event (پیکار کیخسرو) before the compound verbal expression آمد پدید.
segments.s293-g150: ز marks source (“from”), من = “me/I” (agent or source here), جادویها is the plural noun “magical deeds/acts” or “sorceries.” بباید شنید is an impersonal modal construction (بباید = “one must / it is necessary”) + شنید (to be heard / to hear of). So the phrase means “one must hear of the magics from me” or “you should learn of my magic‑deeds.”
segments.s293-g151: بدین داستان در ببارم همی is poetic: بدین (به + این, “with/by this” → “thus/with this”) داستان = “tale/story.” در ببارم is a verbal image using باریدن (to rain/flow) with the 1st‑person stem בبارم = “I let fall / I pour (forth).” همی is the frequentative/continuous particle (“still / I do so”). The whole means “I will thus continue to pour (forth) in this tale” — i.e. “I will keep composing/flowing this story.”
segments.s293-g152: به سنگ اندرون لاله کارم همی: به سنگ اندرون = “into/within the stone” (به = to/into, سنگ اندرون = interior of the stone). لاله کارم is “the tulip of my work” where کارم = کـار + ـم (my work) and لاله is a poetic emblem (tulip/flower/beauty) describing the finest part of the work. همی again marks continuity. Together: “I will place / set the tulip of my work within a gemstone,” a poetic way to say he will enshrine or ornament his composition.
segments.s293-g9: This clause has the finite verb نماند (at the end), a classical past-form meaning 'was left/ remained (not)'. ز گیتی به کان اندرون زر follows the typical Persian ordering of prepositional phrases before the verb: ز گیتی ('from/of the world') + به کان اندرون ('into/at the inner mines') + زر ('gold'). Together they produce the negative existential idea 'no gold remained in the world's inner mines.'
segments.s293-g10: که introduces a subordinate/result clause ('that...'). منشور جود is a compound noun 'charter/manifest of generosity'; ورا is the older clitic form (و + را) meaning 'to him/it'. بر is a directional/prepositional element here ('toward/for'), and نخواند is a classical third-person verb (negative form) used to indicate 'did not/could not call or summon' — in context best understood as 'would summon' or 'called for' in the sense that his generosity called for the gold. Note the verb again comes at the end.
segments.s293-g11: به بزم اندرون is a locative/benefactive phrase ('at/for the inner banquet/court') placed before the verb, a common Persian order. گنج پیدا کند is a verbal phrase where پیدا (here 'appear/bring forth' or 'find') combines with کند (habitual/presentive 'does/makes') to mean 'he produced/displayed/caused the treasure to appear.' The verb کند is present/habitual, indicating repeated action.
segments.s293-g12: چو introduces a temporal clause here ('when'). رزم آیدش is an impersonal/affective construction: آیدش = 'comes upon him' (آید + -ش clitic). رنج بینا کند places the adjective/participial بینا ('made visible' / 'apparent') after the noun رنج ('hardship'), with کند as the causative/result verb at the end: literally 'it makes hardship visible.' The verb-final order and the use of کند for habitual/present causation are typical of Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s293-g13: به بار آورد is a verbal phrase ('brought / bore / caused to carry') with آورد as the main verb at the end. بار here is 'load' or 'bearing' and شاخ دین و خرد is a poetic compound 'the branch (symbol/standard) of religion and wisdom' acting as the object; Persian places these noun phrases before the verb, so the clause reads literally 'he brought/bore the branch of religion and wisdom.'
segments.s293-g14: This clause has گمانش ('his esteem/standing/expectation') as subject, followed by the prepositional phrase به دانش خرد ('by/with the knowledge of wisdom'). پرورد is a classical past/preterite-like form meaning 'nurtured' or 'fostered' (verb at the end). The structure means 'he cultivated his standing/repute through learning and wisdom.' Note Ferdowsi uses nouns like گمان in a broader poetic sense (reputation/esteem).
segments.s293-g15: به اندیشه is an ablative/benefactive phrase here meaning 'by thought/by reflection' placed before the verb. از بی گزندان ('from without harm' = 'free from hurt') is a compound prepositional phrase; گزندان (classical plural-like form of گزند) means 'harm.' بود is the past/copular verb 'was.' The clause reads in normal Persian order: 'By thought he was free from harm.'
segments.s293-g16: This is a straightforward nominal clause: همیشه ('always') precedes the subject پناهش ('his refuge/protection'), then the prepositional phrase به یزدان ('in God'), and the copula بود ('was') at the end. Word order is standard: adverb + subject + prepositional complement + verb, giving 'His refuge was always in God.'
segments.s293-g113: This clause is a wish introduced implicitly by the verb دهد (third-person present used optatively: “may he give”). مرا is the object pronoun + object marker (here split as م + را): “me.” از جهان بی‌نیازی is a prepositional phrase where از governs جهان in the sense “this life/world,” and بی‌نیازی is the noun “freedom/self-sufficiency.”
segments.s293-g114: Parallel clause coordinated by repetition of دهد: میان + noun expresses “among/amongst.” گوان is an old/poetic term here meaning a class/group (rendered idiomatically as “others/people”). سرفرازی is a noun ‘glory, exaltation,’ and دهد repeats the optative “may he give.”
segments.s293-g115: بادا is an optative particle meaning “may it be”; جاوید is the adjective “eternal.” سر و تخت is a paired noun phrase ‘head and throne’ (head/top + throne) with اوی (= his) referring back to the ruler. The whole clause is a wish: “may his head and throne be eternal.”
segments.s293-g116: به کام دلش is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/according to the desire of his heart” (دلش = دل + enclitic ش = his). گردش بخت is a nominal phrase “the turning/circuit of fortune.” اوی again is the possessive/pronominal reference (“his”). The clause continues the optative sense from بادا: “may the turning of his fortune be to his heart’s desire.”
segments.s293-g117: The clause begins with که introducing a relative/nominal clause (“who/that”). داند is the present of ‘to know’ (here descriptive: ‘knows’), and ورا is the old object form “him.” در جهان خود ستود is a phrase “praised him in his own world/realm” (خود = his own). Word order follows classical Persian with object pronouns placed after the verb and possessive خود after جهان.
segments.s293-g118: کسی is “someone/anyone.” کش here functions as an agentive/derivational element (classical/poetic use) linked to the following verb; ستاید is a present/jussive form of ‘praise’ (“may praise” / “praises” depending on context). The final clause که یارد شنود is a relative clause “who hears the friend” (یارد = companion/friend, شنود = he hears). Overall: “Let anyone praise him who hears his friend.”
segments.s293-g119: که introduces the clause (‘for/since’ or a relative ‘that’). The structure is predicative: شاه (subject) + از … برتر است (‘is superior to …’). از governs the two nouns گمان (thought/conjecture) and توان (power/ability), and برتر است is the comparative predicate ‘is superior.’
segments.s293-g120: چو is a simile marker “as/like.” بر تارک مشتری is a prepositional phrase “on the brow of Jupiter” (بر = on). افسر is the noun “helmet,” and است is the copula; the phrase gives a simile: the king’s exaltation is like a helmet upon Jupiter’s brow. تارک is an old/poetic word for the brow/top and appears in inverted/poetic order.
segments.s293-g89: This clause contains two coordinated actions. بداد is an archaic past-form verbal phrase meaning roughly 'he rendered justice' (here used as a short predicate) followed by و 'and'. به بخشش is a prepositional phrase literally 'by/with generosity' (به + noun) that sets the manner, and گرفت is a simple past 3rd-person singular verb 'took/seized'. این جهان is the direct object 'this world' (این = demonstrative + جهان = world). The overall order is literary but follows Persian SOV tendencies: manner phrase + verb + object.
segments.s293-g90: سرش is the noun سر 'head/top' with the clitic ـش 'his' (possessive). برتر آمد is an adjectival predicate where برتر 'more elevated/superior' combines with آمد 'came' (here 'rose/became'), a common Persian pattern meaning 'became superior' or 'rose above'. ز introduces the comparison 'from/than' and شاهنشهان is the plural/poetic form 'the shahanshahs/emperors'.
segments.s293-g91: فروزان is an adjective 'radiant/brilliant' placed before شد, the past of شدن 'to become', forming 'became radiant'. آثار تاریخ اوی is a noun phrase 'the works/traces of his history' where آثار (works/remains) is the head, تاریخ 'history/chronicle' qualifies it, and اوی is the genitive/possessive clitic 'his'. The verb شد follows the adjective in this poetic order to predicate the noun phrase.
segments.s293-g92: که introduces a subordinate optative/wish clause here ('that/may'). جاوید بادا is an optative formula: جاوید 'eternal' + بادا 'may it be' (بادا = optative particle). بن و بیخ اوی is a coordinated noun phrase 'its root and its origin' where اوی is the possessive clitic 'his/its' attached at the end to govern both بن and بیخ.
segments.s293-g93: از آن پس queuing 'from that time/after that' introduces a temporal clause with که 'that/when'. گوشم is گوش + possessive ـم 'my ear'; note the possessive attaches to the noun. شنید is the simple past 'heard' with آن خروش 'that cry/uproar' as its object. The clause thus reads 'from that time when my ear heard that uproar.'
segments.s293-g94: نهادم is a 1st-person past 'I placed/put' (نهادم = نهادم). بر marks the target 'upon/toward', آن تیز آواز is 'that sharp/penetrating voice' (تیز modifying آواز), and گوش here is 'ear' (the body part) — the phrase literally 'I laid/turned my ear to that sharp voice.' The order is poetic but transparent: subject-verb then prepositional target.
segments.s293-g95: بپیوستم is a 1st-person past 'I joined/attached'. این نامه‌بر is 'this letter-carrier/envoy' (نامه‌بر = messenger), and نام اوی is 'his name' where اوی is the possessive clitic. Together the clause means 'I have joined/attached this messenger/epistle to his name' (i.e., appended this to his record).
segments.s293-g96: همه مهتری is 'all/entire preeminence' (همه modifying مهتری). باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)', and فرجام اوی 'his outcome/end' has اوی as the possessive clitic. The phrase is a wish: 'May all preeminence be his fate/outcome.' Word order puts the wished-about noun phrase before باد, which is common in poetic optative expressions.
segments.s293-g129: کند is the 3rd‑person singular present-subjunctive/optative of کردن used here as a wish 'may (it) do/make'. آفرین is a noun/exclamation 'praise, acclamation' and بر + جهاندار شاه means 'to/for the sovereign king' (بر as the preposition 'to/at'). The whole segment expresses an optative clause: 'May praise be made to the sovereign king.'
segments.s293-g130: که introduces a subordinate explanatory clause 'for/that'. بی او is a prepositional phrase 'without him' (بی = without, او = him). مبیناد is an older/poetic present‑subjunctive of 'to appear/be seen' (here 'appear/come before'); کس means 'anyone/no one' depending on context (here 'anyone'), and پیشگاه means 'the presence (of the court)/before the throne'. Together: 'for without him no one appears before the court.'
segments.s293-g131: مر is a dative/ethical 'to me' (i.e. 'in my view'). او را ستاینده literally 'the one who praises him' or 'that which praises him' — ستاینده is a participle/noun 'praiser/praising'. کردار اوست is a copular clause 'is his deed(s)'. The clause therefore means 'In my eyes, what praises him is his deed' → 'To me, his deeds praise him.'
segments.s293-g132: جهان 'the world' is the subject. سر به سر is an idiom 'completely/throughout' (literally 'head to head'). زیر آثار اوست uses زیر in the sense 'subject to/under the authority of' and آثار او 'his works/effects' with ست as the copula 'is'. The whole sentence: 'The whole world, entirely, is under his works.'
segments.s293-g133: چو here functions in the sense 'since/when' introducing a reason. مایه 'means/material' and ندارم 'I do not have' (present negative of داشتن) give 'I have no means'. ثنای ورا = 'for praising him' (ثنای = praise, ورا = to him). Entire segment: 'Since I have no material with which to praise him…'.
segments.s293-g134: نیایش is 'prayer/supplication' and کنم is the 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used here as 'I will do / I make (a prayer)'. خاک پای ورا is a set phrase 'the dust of his foot/feet' (خاک = dust, پای = foot, ورا = to him) meaning humble abasement at his feet. Together: 'I will pray and (be) the dust of his feet.'
segments.s293-g135: زمانه 'the age/era' is the subject; سراسر 'throughout/entire' modifies it. بدو 'to him' (or 'for him') marks the beneficiary, and زنده‌باد is an optative/exclamation 'Long live!' — here a wish that the whole era proclaim or wish him long life. The line is an optative blessing: 'May the whole age cry "Long live to him!"'.
segments.s293-g136: خرد 'wisdom' is the actor wished to act; تخت او را 'his throne' (تخت = throne, او = his, را = object marker). فروزنده 'illuminating/brightening' is a participial adjective/agent and باد is the optative particle 'may' (not the literal 'wind'). Together: 'May wisdom illuminate his throne.'
segments.s293-g185: "که" here is the subordinating/relativizing particle 'that' introducing this explanatory clause. "کین پدر" is a genitive noun phrase (lit. 'hatred of the father' = 'the father's vengeance') with the modifier preceding the head (classical poetic word order, ezāfe often omitted). "باز جست" is a past intransitive verb phrase: "باز" = "again" and "جست" = past of جستن/جهیدن meaning 'sprang up/was rekindled' (an archaic/poetic past form). "از نیا" uses the preposition "از" ('from/of') with "نیا" (here a personal name or ancestor); syntactically it marks the source/target associated with the rekindling.
segments.s293-g186: This is an instrumental phrase introduced by the preposition "به" ('by/with'). "شمشیر" is the first instrument ('the sword'). "و هم چاره و کیمیا" lists additional means: "و" = 'and', "هم" = 'also', "چاره" = 'remedy/expedient', and "کیمیا" = 'alchemy/elixir' — together they specify the ways or means by which the vengeance was pursued.
segments.s293-g187: "نیا را" marks 'Niyâ' as the definite direct object with the particle "را". "بکشت" is an archaic past-form of کشتن (3rd person singular) meaning 'he killed' (the prefixed b- is typical in older Persian past forms). "و خود ایدر نماند" is coordinated: "و" = 'and', "خود" is emphatic 'he himself', "ایدر" = 'here', and "نماند" is the past negative/negative-stem of ماندن meaning 'did not remain' or 'did not stay'.
segments.s293-g188: Subject + predicate clause. "جهان نیز" = 'the world likewise' ("نیز" = also). "منشور او را" is the object phrase: "منشور" = 'inscribed tablet/charter' (or 'inscription'), with "او را" marking it as 'his (inscription)' — in classical usage possession and object marking can co-occur. "نخواند" is the negated past of خواندن (3rd singular) meaning 'did not read/recite', so the clause means 'the world also did not read his inscription.'
segments.s293-g189: "چنین است" is a fixed copular phrase meaning 'thus it is' or 'such is'. "رسم" = 'custom/usage' and "سرای سپنج" is a noun phrase ('the house/household of Sepanj' or a named place); in classical poetry the ezāfe linking these nouns is often omitted, so read it as 'the custom of the house Sepanj.'
segments.s293-g190: "بدان کوش" is an exhortation: classical "بدان" functions adverbially as 'to/for that' or 'attend to that' (here consisting of the directional prefix "ب-" plus the following element) and "کوش" is the imperative/ exhortative stem meaning 'strive/endeavor'. "تا دور مانی ز رنج" gives the purpose: "تا" = 'so that', "دور" = 'far', "مانی" is a jussive/subjunctive form 'you may remain' (classical/poetic second-person subjunctive of ماندن), and "ز رنج" = 'from suffering' ("ز" = 'from', "رنج" = 'suffering'). The whole clause means 'strive at that so that you may remain far from suffering.'
segments.s293-g161: یکی را uses the object/ topical marker را after یکی to single out 'one person' (equivalent to 'for one' or 'as for one'); همه بهره is a nominal phrase 'the whole/entire share/portion'; شهد است و قند is a simple copular clause ('is honey and sugar') where است is the copula and the following و + noun adds a second coordinated predicate noun. Word order is poetic but literal: 'one (as for) — whole portion — is honey and sugar.'
segments.s293-g162: This is a nominal list of the items making up that 'portion': تن آسانی 'bodily ease/comfort', و ناز 'and pampering/indulgence', و بخت بلند 'and lofty/high fortune'. There is no new verb here; these nouns are understood as completing or exemplifying the 'portion' predicated by the previous clause (the copula from the prior segment carries over).
segments.s293-g163: یکی ز او here marks 'one (person) from/of him' or 'one of them; because of him' with ز used poetically in a causal/relational sense; همه‌ساله is an adverbial 'yearly / year after year' modifying the succeeding predicate; با درد و رنج is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with pain and suffering' that sets the circumstances for the change described next.
segments.s293-g164: شده is the perfect/passive participle 'has become', followed by the adjective تنگدل 'narrow‑hearted/melancholic', so the structure is stative: 'has become narrow‑hearted'. در + سرای سپنج is a locative phrase 'in the house/room of سپنج'; note that the lemma/senses given for سرای in the data do not match its usual meaning 'house/abode', so that entry is marked below as unmatched. The whole segment states the resulting state and its locale.
segments.s293-g165: یکی را again topicalizes 'one person'. همه رفتن is a nominalized/collective expression 'all (is) going' or 'everything proceeds/falls into' and رفتن is used in the sense 'to go/ to be driven into'; اندر is the older preposition 'into/in', and نهیب here means a loud cry/uproar (poetic use). The clause thus describes that this person's lot is to be plunged into tumult.
segments.s293-g166: گهی ... گهی is a correlative pair meaning 'sometimes ... sometimes' (alternation). در فراز 'on high / at the summit' and در نشیب 'in descent / in the low place' give the alternating locations or states; the preposition در governs these nouns. This adverbial pair qualifies the preceding verb phrase (where the 'going into uproar' occurs alternately high and low).
segments.s293-g167: چنین 'thus' sets the manner; پروراند is a past transitive 'caused to rear/raised' and together with همی (an archaic/poetic particle meaning 'still/ever' or giving a continuous/Habitual sense) forms 'thus did Time/Fate ever nurture'. روزگار is the agent/subject 'time' or 'fate' in this personification. The clause is compact and poetic in word order (verb before subject).
segments.s293-g168: فزون آمد is a two‑word predicate 'increased / came to be more' (فزون 'more/increasing' + آمد 'came'); از introduces the standard of comparison ('more than'); رنگ گل is 'the color/hue of the flower'; رنج خار literally 'the suffering of the thorn(s)' or 'thorny suffering' — the phrase means that hardship/affliction (the thorn) grew greater than the flower's color (i.e., hardship outweighed beauty). The comparative sense ('more than') is conveyed by فزون آمد ... از.
segments.s293-g81: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. 'پنج از سر سال' is a prepositional phrase with از + سر 'from the head/top (of the year/life)', i.e. 'five (years) from the head'; شستم is a perfective first-person verb 'I washed/cleansed' used here metaphorically for 'had passed/been removed', and نشست is the following perfective verb 'sat' (I sat down). The clause order places the temporal phrase before the verbs, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s293-g82: من is the explicit subject 'I'. اندر + نشیب is a locative phrase 'in the descent' (i.e. in a declining state). و coordinates the two predicative parts. سرم here is 'sar + -am' (my head) elliptical for 'my head (was)...' and سوی پست is a directional phrase 'toward the low/despised' (سوی = toward, پست = low/despised). The clause after نشست is verbless (elliptical): the earlier verb 'نشست' governs the state, and the phrases explain where and how the speaker sat.
segments.s293-g83: رخ 'face/cheek' is the subject. لاله گون is a compound adjective: لاله (tulip) + گون (‑like / ‑colored) = 'tulip-colored' (i.e. rosy). گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned'. بر سان = بر + سان is a poetic simile 'on the likeness of / like', and کاه is the simile's object 'straw'. Word order (subject — predicate — simile) reflects Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s293-g84: چو here is a temporal/comparative conjunction 'when/as'. کافور is the noun 'camphor'. شد is the simple past 'became' (used as main verb). رنگ 'color/complexion' is the patient/theme, مشک 'musk' names the reference color, and سیاه is the adjective 'black'. The clause uses an inverted, condensed poetic order: 'when camphor (i.e. something white) had become black in the color of musk'.
segments.s293-g85: بدان گه is an archaic/time phrase 'at that time' (بدan/ب + دان/ان + گه in poetic formation); که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when'. بد سال is 'bad/ill-fated year' (بد qualifying سال). پنجاه و هفت is the compound numeral 'fifty-seven' with و linking tens and units. The whole segment sets the temporal and descriptive frame: 'at that time, in the unlucky year fifty-seven...'.
segments.s293-g86: نوانتر is a comparative/adverbial form (نوان 'new/youthful' + ‑تر 'more') meaning 'more youthful/renewed'. شدم is the past 'I became'. چون here functions as 'when/as' introducing a subordinate temporal clause. جوانی is the noun 'youth'. برفت is an older/poetic past 'bar‑raft' = 'went/was gone' (archaic form of رفت), meaning 'had gone/passed'. Together: 'I became more youthful when/though youth had gone' (poetic, somewhat paradoxical).
segments.s293-g87: فریدون is the proper name (subject). بیدار + دل is a compound adjective/noun phrase 'awake-hearted' or 'his heart awakened' (بیدار modifies دل). زنده شد is a two-word past construction 'became alive' (زنده 'alive' + شد 'became'). The sequence states a change of state: Fereydun's heart woke and he came to life.
segments.s293-g88: زمان و زمین is a coordinated subject 'time and earth'. پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'before him / in his presence'. بنده شد is 'became a servant' (بنده as predicate noun 'servant/humble one' + شد 'became'), so the phrase means 'time and earth became servants before him' — a poetic way to say all things were subject to him.
segments.s293-g73: همی داشتم is a literary progressive past: همی (continuative) + داشتم (first-person past auxiliary) = "I kept on / I was continually". تا introduces the temporal limit "until," کی is the interrogative/indefinite "when," and آید پدید (آید = come; پدید = forth/appear) together mean "might come forth / appear." The clause thus reads literally: "I kept waiting until when he might come forth."
segments.s293-g74: جوادی is a substantive/adjective meaning "a generous one." که is the relative pronoun "whose/that." جودش is the noun جود "generosity" with the possessive suffix -ش "his." نخواهد is the negative present/future of خواستن here meaning "will not want / will not need," and کلید is the direct object "key." Word order is somewhat compressed by poetic style: "a generous man whose generosity will not need a key."
segments.s293-g75: نگهبان is the noun 'guardian/protector.' The phrase repeats نگهبان for emphasis: "guardian of religion and guardian of the crown." In Persian poetry the usual ezāfe is elided, so the two nouns (دین, تاج) follow without an overt linking particle.
segments.s293-g76: فروزنده is a participle/adjective meaning "illuminating / making shine" and here modifies the two following nouns. افسر (helmet) و تخت عاج (throne of ivory) are the objects illuminated; عاج qualifies تخت as "ivory throne." The participle precedes the nouns in this poetic ordering.
segments.s293-g77: به رزم is the prepositional phrase "in battle/for combat." دلیران (the brave, plural) specifies the opponents or the context, and توانا بود is the predicate "he was capable / powerful." بود is the past copula here, so the clause reads "he was able/powerful in battle against the brave."
segments.s293-g78: به چون و چرا is an idiomatic pairing: چون (here 'how') و چرا ('why') together mean dealing with reasons, objections, or particulars. نیز = "also / too," and دانا بود = "he was wise." The clause means that, besides martial ability, he was also wise in matters of reasoning and judgment.
segments.s293-g79: چنین سال بگذاشتم is a first‑person past statement: چنین "thus/so," سال "years," and بگذاشتم (prefixed past of گذاشتن) meaning "I spent/ I passed / I left (years)." شست و پنج is the numeral "sixty‑five" (classical form); here the poem lists the number after the verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.s293-g80: به درویشی و زندگانی به رنج: the first به marks the sphere 'in dervishhood,' و links 'and,' زندگانی = 'life/livelihood,' and the final به رنج = 'in suffering / with hardship.' The doubled به frames the circumstances in which those sixty‑five years were spent.
segments.s293-g97: "از آن پس" is an adverbial phrase meaning "from that time / thereafter"; the remainder is a compact nominal clause with an implied verb (e.g. رفت "went"). "تن جانور" is a genitive/attributive juxtaposition meaning "the body of the beast/animal" and "خاک راست" places the destination (خاک = earth/dust) followed by راست as a postpositive adverb meaning "straight/directly." In this poetic word order the verb is omitted and the time, subject, destination and manner are expressed by juxtaposed nouns and adjectives.
segments.s293-g98: The doubled "روان روان" is poetic repetition referring to the soul/spirit (روان) — here used emphatically to mark the departing spirit. "معدن پاک" is a noun + adjective "pure source/seat" (معدن = source/mine, پاک = pure). Final "راست" again functions as a postpositive adverb "straight/directly" indicating the soul's destination. The verb of motion is again omitted and supplied from context.
segments.s293-g99: "همان" is a demonstrative/emphatic "that very / the same." It introduces the noun "نیزه" (spear), followed by two epithets placed after the noun in classical/poetic order: "بخشنده" (generous, giver) and "دادگر" (just). This is a nominal phrase of apposition/epithet; no finite verb appears here.
segments.s293-g100: "کز" is a relative particle meaning "from whom / from which"; "اوی" is the classical dependent form of the pronoun referring back to the person/thing just named (here: 'of him' / 'from him'); "است" is the copula "is"; "پیدا" is predicative meaning "manifest / apparent"; "به گیتی" is a locative phrase "in the world" (به + گیتی); and "هنر" is the predicate noun meaning "art/skill or excellence." Word order is relative clause: "from whom is manifest in the world excellence."
segments.s293-g101: "که" here introduces a wish/result clause meaning roughly "so that / may it be that." "باشد" is the copula in an optative/subjunctive sense ("may be"); "به پیری" is a temporal expression "in (my) old age"; "مرا" (written here as م + را) is the first-person object "for me" (م = me, را = object marker); and "دستگیر" is a substantive meaning "helper, succorer." The Persian order places the copula before the time phrase and the beneficiary before the noun "دستگیر"; the whole means "that there be for me a helper in old age."
segments.s293-g102: A nominal apposition naming who the helper should be: "خداوند" here means "lord/sovereign" (not specifically the monotheistic God in this enumeration), followed by a series of possessions/titles joined by "و": "شمشیر" (sword), "تاج" (crown), "سریر" (throne). The phrase functions as predicate nominative to "دستگیر" above: "may the lord of sword, crown, and throne be my helper."
segments.s293-g103: A coordinated list: each phrase "خداوند X" means "lord/sovereign of X." Here "خداوند هند" = "lord of India" and after the conjunction "و" the repeated "خداوند چین" = "lord of China." Repetition of خداوند is a rhetorical/device common in epic lists.
segments.s293-g104: Continuation of the list: "خداوند ایران" = "lord of Iran," coordinated with "و" and then "توران زمین," where "زمین" (land) postmodifies "توران" (Turan) forming "the land of Turan." The initial "خداوند" can be understood to govern both coordinated objects (Iran and Turan's land).
segments.s293-g169: هر آنگه که ... is a temporal subordinate clause meaning “whenever/whenever it is that ...”. سال (subject) is followed by the past verb آمد (آمد, ‘came/arrived’) — classical word order places the verb after its noun. به marks the point reached (literally “to/at”), so به شست is a time-point phrase (‘at sixty’); the verb آمد is third person past.
segments.s293-g170: بباید + infinitive (کشیدن) expresses necessity: “one must [do X]”. کشیدن is the infinitive ‘to pull/draw’ and takes دست as its direct object (literally ‘to pull the hand’ = ‘withdraw the hand’). ز بیشیت is a prepositional phrase ‘from excess’ (ز = from; بیشیت = excess). Word order is modal + infinitive + object phrase.
segments.s293-g171: This is a negative general statement: برنگذرد (negative verb ‘does not pass/does not exceed’) precedes the subject as is common in poetic word order. ز هفتاد marks the limit ‘past seventy’; بس کسی functions as an adverbial/quantifier indicating that (very) few/none pass beyond that age.
segments.s293-g172: ز دوران چرخ is a genitive-like temporal/source phrase ‘from the turns/cycles of the wheel (of fate)’. آزمودم is first-person past ‘I experienced/tested’. بسی is a postpositive adverb meaning ‘much/many’ or ‘plenty’ and intensifies the verb.
segments.s293-g173: وگر introduces a conditional ‘and if / but if’. بگذرد is a present/subjunctive third-person ‘it passes’ (here ‘should pass’). آن همه is the neutral demonstrative + quantifier ‘all that’; بتریست is a predicative clause meaning ‘it is worse’ (بتری- + -ست, an archaic/predicative formation).
segments.s293-g174: بر آن زندگانی = ‘over/about that life’ (بر = over/about, آن = that, زندگانی = life). بباید + گریست again expresses necessity: ‘one must weep/lament’ (گریست = lament/weep). The construction is impersonal/general: ‘one ought to lament that life.’
segments.s293-g175: اگر introduces a conditional ‘if’. دام ماهی is a noun phrase ‘a fish‑trap’ (دام = trap, ماهی = fish; here ماهی is literal ‘fish’ used to specify trap type). بدی is an adjective ‘bad/ill’ qualifying the trap. سال شست is a time expression ‘in the sixtieth year’ (شست here appears as an archaic/poetic time-marker; the exact lexical reading is uncertain in the manuscript tradition).
segments.s293-g176: خردمند is the subject ‘the wise (man)’. از او marks source ‘from it’ (i.e., from the trap/situation). یافتی is a past-tense verb ‘found’ (classical form; here used with third-person subject). راه جست is an idiom ‘to find a way/way out’ (راه = way; جست = sought/found/seized the way). The overall meaning: the wise will find a way out of such a trap.

sentences.s293-s61: I have done one act of servitude, O king, which remains of me in the world as a memorial.
sentences.s293-s62: Even prosperous buildings are ruined by rain and by the sun's rays.
sentences.s293-s63: I then erected, following the verse, a lofty palace that no harm may come to from wind or rain.
sentences.s293-s64: Years will pass over this letter‑bearer; one who has wisdom will continually read it.
sentences.s293-s25: For he is the adornment of the revolving heavens, and in the inner banquet he is the generous cloud.
sentences.s293-s26: May there be wisdom, good repute and justice; may the world never be without a head and his crown.
sentences.s293-s27: There are troops and heart and treasure and order; likewise there are battle and feasting and wine (pleasure) and revelry.
sentences.s293-s28: A certain carpet was spread in the world, whose mark may never become hidden.
sentences.s293-s21: That in the world's assembly he shines brightly; in battle the inner part of the mountain is clad in armor.
sentences.s293-s22: Abu'l-Qasim, that brave sovereign — where can he lay a grave beyond the lion's claw?
sentences.s293-s23: Sovereign Mahmud — in that battle he brings the heads of the proud into his circle.
sentences.s293-s24: May he be king as long as the world endures; may his lofty star be the moon's diadem.
sentences.s293-s53: The beautiful, high‑minded lord — messages and reproaches are far from him.
sentences.s293-s54: At his voice the stony mountain rends; in the sea, the whale, and on land, the leopard.
sentences.s293-s55: Be it a dinar before his banquet or dust, because of his generosity his heart has no fear.
sentences.s293-s56: The sovereign Mahmud Khurshidfash — in battle he is a lion, a brandisher of the sword.
sentences.s293-s9: When he takes the frontier with a keen sword, he stirs up a resurrection throughout the world.
sentences.s293-s10: He takes from the enemy and bestows to the friend; God, the victorious, is his ally.
sentences.s293-s11: Know that that sword-striking, gem-scattering hand seeks nothing from the world except a token.
sentences.s293-s12: who in his oceanic banquet summoned the heavens; in battle he displayed the inward lion, sun-faced.
sentences.s293-s13: He gives testimony on the earth — on soil and water — and likewise upon the heavens the sun's spring.
sentences.s293-s14: No king like him has ever been seen in battle — neither in generosity and effort, nor in renown and shame.
sentences.s293-s15: If love were mixed with hatred, the stars would shrink from his anger.
sentences.s293-s16: His body is powerful, and his army so numerous that there is no way for the wind through their midst.
sentences.s293-s69: His heart, joyful like the lush spring, ever through this turning of time.
sentences.s293-s70: Because of him the assembly's heart rejoiced; in every task (he was) victorious and masterful in speech.
sentences.s293-s71: As long as the heavens revolve, Jupiter's influence rested in him.
sentences.s293-s72: May our king be (blest) with rank and dignity; may the evil eye be far from him and may he be without need.
sentences.s293-s77: Now I have found a pen greater than that with which I earlier wove the core of speech.
sentences.s293-s78: The ordeal has set (or placed) two eyes — sometimes joy, sometimes pain and anger.
sentences.s293-s79: It is a marvel that within this lapis-lazuli dome such a heart, full of burning grief and sorrow, should remain.
sentences.s293-s80: It was thus for a long time; do not remain astonished at your novelty.
sentences.s293-s29: Where the carpet serves as throne and resting-place — the seat of Nasr ibn Ahmad.
sentences.s293-s30: For such a calm kingship is liked (or esteemed); wisdom in the minds of the illustrious is good.
sentences.s293-s31: There were no kings like a village‑headman; he observed religion and (acted) with generosity and wise counsel.
sentences.s293-s32: Frank of tongue, open of heart and clean‑handed, a worshipper — indeed a God‑worshipping (man/king).
sentences.s293-s1: May praise from God be upon that king, for the crown, the throne, and the jewel take pride in him.
